steps.description: Set out what must be proved
steps.explanation: Both sides involve $\cot^2\theta$ and $\cos^2\theta$, so the plan is to simplify each side to a single fraction in $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ and check they agree.

steps.description: Express cot squared in sine and cosine
steps.explanation: Cotangent is cosine over sine, so squaring gives $\dfrac{\cos^2\theta}{\sin^2\theta}$; this converts everything to the two basic functions.
We work on the left-hand side first, replacing $\cot^2\theta$ with its sine-cosine form.
Both terms contain $\cos^2\theta$, so taking it out as a common factor isolates a simpler bracket to work on.

steps.description: Combine the bracket over a common denominator
steps.explanation: Writing $1$ as $\dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{\sin^2\theta}$ lets the bracket combine into a single fraction.
Rearranging $\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta\equiv1$ replaces $1-\sin^2\theta$ with $\cos^2\theta$, which is the crucial simplification.
Replacing the numerator of the bracket turns the left-hand side into a clean product of squares.
Multiplying the two cosine factors gives $\cos^4\theta$ over $\sin^2\theta$, a single tidy fraction.
We treat the right-hand side independently, again writing $\cot^2\theta$ as $\dfrac{\cos^2\theta}{\sin^2\theta}$.
Both sides reduce to the identical expression, which establishes the identity.
Because $\cot\theta$ and the denominators require $\sin\theta\neq0$, the identity holds for all $\theta$ where it is defined.
With $\cot^2 60^\circ=\tfrac13$ and $\cos^2 60^\circ=\tfrac14$, both sides equal $\tfrac{1}{12}$, confirming the result.
Each side simplifies to $\dfrac{\cos^4\theta}{\sin^2\theta}$, so the identity is proved.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Both sides simplify to $\dfrac{\cos^4\theta}{\sin^2\theta}$: the left via $\cos^2\theta\big(\tfrac{1}{\sin^2\theta}-1\big)=\cos^2\theta\cdot\tfrac{\cos^2\theta}{\sin^2\theta}$, so $\cot^2\theta-\cos^2\theta\equiv\cot^2\theta\cos^2\theta$.
